title: Downstream Datasets Make Surprisingly Good Pretraining Corpora

structured.summary: The paper investigates the efficacy of self-pretraining, a method that uses the same downstream data for both pretraining and finetuning, in NLP models like ELECTRA and RoBERTa. It demonstrates that self-pretraining can match or surpass traditional pretraining using large-scale corpora on several datasets while raising questions about the necessity of massive upstream data.

strengths.evidence: The study of self-pretraining in NLP draws parallels to similar explorations in computer vision and challenges conventional reliance on upstream corpora.
The method could reduce dependence on large-scale upstream corpora, alleviating issues around intellectual property and offensive content in data.

weaknesses.evidence: Section 2 cites previous work in computer vision by El-Nouby et al. (2021) and He et al. (2022) that has already explored self-pretraining techniques.
Comparisons focus primarily on traditional large datasets like BookWiki, lacking engagement with other state-of-the-art techniques (Section 3).
The evaluation uses 10 datasets without detailed analysis of how dataset complexity, size, or label distribution affect performance (Section 3).
The experimental setup section omits detailed configurations of hyperparameters, constraints on reproducibility (Section 3).
Results are primarily based on GLUE scores without exploring broader task diversity beyond this benchmark (Results section).
The heuristic for reordering shuffled sentences lacks a robust theoretical backing or detailed analysis (Section 1).
Impact of confounding variables like dataset size or task difficulty is not adequately controlled or discussed (Section 3).
Claims that pretraining objectives primarily drive performance gains (Section 1) lack concrete experimental support.
Discussion on cross-validation techniques is absent, raising concerns about robustness and validity of results (Section 3).
Conclusions in the paper suggest broad applicability to the entire NLP domain without diverse task or language evidence (Conclusions section).
No discussion is present regarding the computational cost or efficiency of the self-pretraining method compared to traditional approaches.
The experimental scope appears limited, with no evidence on how self-pretraining scales with different dataset sizes and complexities.

questions.question: How does the method's computational cost compare to traditional pretraining?
questions.motivation: Understanding the cost-benefit analysis is crucial for practical adoption and scalability of self-pretraining over traditional methods.

critique_points.point_001: Lack of Novelty in Self-Pretraining. Section 2 cites previous work in computer vision by El-Nouby et al. (2021) and He et al. (2022) that has already explored self-pretraining techniques
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient Comparative Baseline. Comparisons focus primarily on traditional large datasets like BookWiki, lacking engagement with other state-of-the-art techniques (Section 3)
critique_points.point_003: Weak Evaluation Methodology. The evaluation uses 10 datasets without detailed analysis of how dataset complexity, size, or label distribution affect performance (Section 3)
critique_points.point_004: Limited Transparency and Reproducibility. The experimental setup section omits detailed configurations of hyperparameters, constraints on reproducibility (Section 3)
critique_points.point_005: Overreliance on GLUE Benchmark. Results are primarily based on GLUE scores without exploring broader task diversity beyond this benchmark (Results section)
critique_points.point_006: Heuristic Approach Lacks Theoretical Justification. The heuristic for reordering shuffled sentences lacks a robust theoretical backing or detailed analysis (Section 1)
critique_points.point_007: Confounding Factors. Impact of confounding variables like dataset size or task difficulty is not adequately controlled or discussed (Section 3)
critique_points.point_008: Ambiguous Claims About Objective Primacy. Claims that pretraining objectives primarily drive performance gains (Section 1) lack concrete experimental support
critique_points.point_009: Inadequate Cross-Validation. Discussion on cross-validation techniques is absent, raising concerns about robustness and validity of results (Section 3)
critique_points.point_010: Overgeneralization to Broader NLP Community. Conclusions in the paper suggest broad applicability to the entire NLP domain without diverse task or language evidence (Conclusions section)
critique_points.point_011: Lack of Analysis on Cost-Effectiveness. No discussion is present regarding the computational cost or efficiency of the self-pretraining method compared to traditional approaches
critique_points.point_012: Scalability Concerns. The experimental scope appears limited, with no evidence on how self-pretraining scales with different dataset sizes and complexities
